_disclaimer: SPECULATIVE discretionary research, NOT a backtested edge. Live snapshot, not point-in-time. Individual-stock survivorship applies. Verdicts are conviction calls for human research, not signals. Manual trades only.

ideas.thesis: Down ~41% from its high and sitting at the bottom 5% of its 52w range, yet the business is re-accelerating, not breaking: Q1 revenue +16% to $12.25B, net income up to $5.28B, EPS $1.23 vs $0.76 expected (a +61% blowout), operating margin 32%. The de-rate came from a 'priced-for-perfection' multiple plus soft near-term guidance and Reed Hastings stepping down as chair — sentiment, not fundamentals. Analysts stay Buy (64) with ~+45% to target, ROE ~17% and rising forward EPS. Classic overcorrection in the only scaled, profitable global streamer.
ideas.key_risk: Still the most expensive name in the cohort (EV/sales >30x) — if subscriber/ARPU growth matures or the recent M&A interest (lost Roku bid to Fox, Lionsgate chatter) turns into a value-destroying acquisition, the premium compresses further.

news.headline: Netflix quarterly sales beat hopes but soft outlook sends shares lower (net income $5.28B, EPS $1.23 vs $0.76 est)
Omdia: Netflix to Reach 400 Million Subscribers by 2031, Maintaining Global Streaming Lead Despite Industry Consolidation
Netflix Flexes 'M&A Muscle' As Bidding Contests Spook Wall Street (lost Roku to Fox)

competitors.name: Walt Disney
competitors.note: streaming only recently profitable; dragged by linear TV decline

competitors.name: Warner Bros. Discovery
competitors.note: high debt, shrinking; splitting the company to survive

competitors.name: Spotify
competitors.note: growing but thin margins; different (audio) niche

ideas.competitor_edge: NFLX is the only streamer combining scale (~300M+ subs), ~28% net margin and real free cash flow; rivals are either burning cash, shrinking linear, or sub-scale — so a streaming-sector recovery accrues to NFLX first.

ideas.thesis: A Dividend-King medtech down ~33% and basing — but the chart has stopped falling (1M roughly flat, turning up) and the business is intact: four diversified segments (devices, diagnostics, nutrition, pharma), FreeStyle Libre CGM running ~$2B/quarter with 7M+ users into a structurally growing diabetes market, plus MitraClip/TriClip structural-heart growth. Analysts are unanimously constructive (31 Buy, 0 Sell) with ~+44% to target — and even the LOW target ($92) sits above today's price. Q1 was a small beat; forward EPS steps up. This looks like a sector-rotation de-rate of a quality compounder, not a broken thesis.
ideas.key_risk: Growth is steady-not-explosive; DexCom competes hard in CGM and ongoing NEC infant-formula litigation is a tail risk. The 'blow-up' here is a re-rating, not a moonshot.

news.headline: Should You Buy Abbott Labs Stock Hand Over Fist Before July 16? (Libre ~$2B/qtr, $9.57B FCF in 2025, 50+ yr dividend grower)
Abbott diversification cushions single-product risk across devices, diagnostics, nutrition and pharma

competitors.name: Medtronic
competitors.note: larger device peer, slower grower, less diversified into diagnostics/nutrition

competitors.name: DexCom
competitors.note: pure-play CGM rival to Libre; faster growth but far pricier and single-product

competitors.name: Stryker
competitors.note: premium ortho/device grower; quality but no diagnostics/nutrition ballast

ideas.competitor_edge: ABT offers MDT-like stability with faster, more diversified growth (Libre + structural heart) at a cheaper multiple than DXCM/SYK — the lowest-risk way to own the diabetes-tech and structural-heart themes.

ideas.thesis: The largest dislocation in the shortlist: down ~58% from peak / ~40% YTD to a fresh 52w low, even though fiscal-Q1 results set records — AI/Data ARR ~$3.4B (+200% y/y), Agentforce alone past $1.2B ARR, seat counts still growing, and a ~+24% EPS beat. Consensus is Buy (73) with ~+75% to target and forward EPS rising; FCF is strong and the buyback generous. The market is pricing CRM as an AI loser; the data says it is monetizing agents. At a high-single/low-double-digit forward P/E this is the cheapest scaled SaaS leader.
ideas.key_risk: It is still a falling knife (-8.6% in the last 5 days, no confirmed base yet) and the bear case is structural: if agentic AI compresses seat-based SaaS faster than CRM can convert it to usage revenue, the de-rate is justified. Bernstein already rates it Underperform ($173). Size accordingly; wait for a base.

news.headline: Salesforce Stock's Sell-Off Keeps Getting Worse. Is It Finally Time to Buy? (AI ARR tripled, fresh 52w low, business setting records)
Salesforce to Buy AI Agent Platform Fin for $3.6 Billion (Agentforce $1.2B ARR, tripled y/y)
Salesforce upgraded to Buy from Neutral at Monness Crespi — valuation 'compelling' after 58% drop from peak

competitors.name: Microsoft (Dynamics/Copilot)
competitors.note: the key AI competitor; far richer multiple and higher margin

competitors.name: ServiceNow
competitors.note: faster grower but priced for perfection — opposite of CRM's setup

competitors.name: Oracle
competitors.note: cloud/apps peer; cheaper than NOW, more expensive than CRM

ideas.competitor_edge: Among large-cap enterprise SaaS, CRM trades at the steepest discount to its own growth and FCF while posting the fastest AI-ARR ramp (Agentforce) — if agentic AI proves additive rather than cannibalistic, CRM has the most multiple to recover.

ideas.thesis: A genuine early-stage turnaround: down ~44% from its high to a level it last saw a decade ago, with the first hard evidence the CEO-Hill 'Win Now' plan is working — North America revenue +3% with wholesale +11%, and February sell-through grew across every channel for the first time in two years. Q3 EPS beat by ~20%, analysts hold a Buy (36) with ~+35% upside, and forward EPS is set to trough then recover. This is the MU/Dell-style 'left-for-dead leader regaining shelf space' pattern.
ideas.key_risk: The recovery is early and unproven: ROE has collapsed to ~4% and gross margin is being squeezed by U.S. tariffs, Greater China revenue is guided down ~20%, and RBC just cut Nike to Sector Perform ($50), pushing the payoff to 2027. June 30 earnings are a binary catalyst (~10% implied move).

news.headline: Headed Into Earnings, Nike Stock Trades Lower Than a Decade Ago — But Is the Turnaround Finally Working? (NA sell-through grew across all channels in Feb)
Nike Turnaround Could Take Longer Despite New Growth Drivers — RBC cuts to Sector Perform, target to $50
Moderately bullish options activity in Nike with shares up 2.75% ahead of June 30 earnings

competitors.name: Deckers (Hoka)
competitors.note: share-taker with higher margin and growth — part of why NKE de-rated

competitors.name: adidas
competitors.note: already further along its own recovery; trades at a premium to NKE

ideas.competitor_edge: NKE is the scale leader (brand, supply chain, sports marketing) trading at the cheapest multiple in the group while challengers (DECK, ONON) command premium valuations — the asymmetry is mean-reversion if Hill restores demand, but the challengers' share gains are the live risk.

ideas.thesis: The most beaten-down name (~-49% from high, bottom 2% of range) and statistically the highest-quality (ROE ~32%, net margin ~37%, strong FCF, rising EPS). Q2 beat on +13% revenue with 150M+ new Acrobat/Express users and AI-first ARR (Firefly/Express) tripling past $500M. On the numbers alone it screams value.
ideas.key_risk: The decline is at least partly justified, which is why this is CAUTION not CONFIRM: (1) a real structural question — generative AI (Canva, OpenAI/Midjourney image gen) threatens the Creative Cloud moat; (2) management just chose to deliberately slow near-term revenue by ~$500M via a freemium pivot, and the stock fell ~25% on 'blowout' results; (3) a leadership vacuum — both the CEO and the CFO are departing within months; (4) analysts are split (29 Hold, 4 Sell) and Evercore just moved to In Line. Needs the freemium bet and AI monetization to prove out before conviction.

news.headline: Adobe's Stock Fell After Reporting Blowout Results. Blame Friction. (+13% revenue, but deliberate $500M near-term revenue slowdown; stock -25%)
Adobe raises full-year sales guidance but CFO departure rocks shares (CFO Dan Durn leaving months after CEO signaled exit)
Adobe Poised to Outperform Peers on AI Growth, Cloud Synergies — RBC, target $285

competitors.name: Intuit
competitors.note: another AI-exposed software compounder, but no comparable disruption overhang

competitors.name: Microsoft (Designer/Copilot)
competitors.note: bundles AI creative tools that pressure Adobe's consumer funnel

competitors.name: Salesforce
competitors.note: fellow de-rated SaaS leader — but CRM's AI story is additive, Adobe's is defensive

ideas.competitor_edge: ADBE is the cheapest (~13x forward EPS) and highest-margin of the software cohort, but the discount exists because it faces the most acute AI-disruption question — so the 'edge' is conditional on Adobe out-innovating free AI tools, which is not yet proven.

ideas.thesis: Screened in as beaten-down (~-32% from high) and cheap (~10x forward, ~7% dividend), but the decline is fundamentally justified — this is a value trap, not a coiled spring. VETO.
ideas.key_risk: Structural, not cyclical: Q3 EPS MISSED by ~12% and revenue fell ~8% with net earnings down ~52%; North America retail volumes are worsening (~-4%), 8 of 10 top brands are in volume decline, the Blue Buffalo pet business needs a reset, and Jefferies sees 'no visible near-term catalysts' for the FY27 inflection. Earnings have fallen below the dividend, putting the ~7% payout under review. Consensus is only Hold with ~+12% upside, forward EPS is flat-to-down, and the secular pressure on center-store packaged food (private label, GLP-1 demand) is real. No blow-up catalyst — the cheapness is the market correctly pricing a slow decliner.

news.headline: General Mills faces mounting headwinds as volume declines and profit pressures weigh on recovery (NAR volumes -4%, 8 of 10 brands declining, no near-term catalysts)
General Mills Volume Strains And Pet Reset Put Dividend Under Review (earnings fell below dividend payments)
General Mills and Campbell's Both Pay ~7% Dividends — Which Is Safer? (revenue -8%, net earnings -52%, EPS below the dividend)

competitors.name: Kraft Heinz
competitors.note: same secular volume decline; cheap for the same bad reasons

competitors.name: Conagra
competitors.note: another low-growth packaged-food name under volume pressure

competitors.name: Kellanova
competitors.note: snacking tilt gives slightly better growth than GIS

ideas.competitor_edge: None worth owning for a blow-up — the entire center-store packaged-food cohort is under the same secular volume pressure, and GIS is not differentiated enough (worsening volumes, dividend at risk) to be the one that re-rates. The honest call is to pass on the whole group.
